Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore on 16 August 2004.
From its incorporation, Ms Annie Chen Liping was a director and major shareholder of Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd and acted as its main decision-maker.
At inception, Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd conducted business organising events to introduce investors from China to potential business opportunities and investments in Singapore.
The business focus of Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd later shifted to property development and building construction, similar to the business of China Heping Construction (Far East) Pte Ltd.

China Heping Construction (Far East) Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore on 13 June 2001 as a subsidiary of Tianjin Heping Construction Group Co Ltd, and Ms Annie Chen Liping was its managing director.

Tianjin Heping Construction Group Co Ltd and Fong Yun Thai Association signed a Cooperation Agreement on 9 July 2009 to jointly develop a residential housing project at 33 Holland Link, with construction work to be carried out by China Heping Construction (Far East) Pte Ltd.

Under the 9 July 2009 Cooperation Agreement, the project cost was stated as S$115 million, the expected completion period was two and a half years, and 27 of 82 semi-detached units were to be allocated to Fong Yun Thai Association and 55 units to Tianjin Heping Construction Group Co Ltd.

The Cooperation Agreement dated 9 July 2009 between Fong Yun Thai Association and Tianjin Heping Construction Group Co Ltd stated that matters not discussed would be negotiated separately and that the agreement would become effective on the date of signing.
On 16 July 2009, ATI Architects issued a letter of award engaging China Heping Construction (Far East) Pte Ltd to undertake excavation works at 33 Holland Link for the project.
Excavation works at the 33 Holland Link site commenced on or around 17 August 2009, carried out by China Heping Construction (Far East) Pte Ltd.

A document titled Joint Venture Agreement dated 23 September 2009 was prepared in English identifying Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd as the developer appointed by Tianjin Heping Construction Group Co Ltd and naming three of the four land trustees as counterparties.

The 23 September 2009 Joint Venture Agreement recorded the parties' intention to enter into a joint venture for the 33 Holland Link project, including scope, completion period and project management details, but it was not executed by all named parties.

Between November 2009 and January 2010, several further drafts of a more detailed Joint Venture Agreement between Fong Yun Thai Association and Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd were prepared and exchanged but not executed.

On 4 December 2009, Mr Ow Chun Ming emailed Ms Annie Chen Liping proposing to become a 50% shareholder in Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd, with Clydesbuilt Group and Tianjin Heping Construction Group Co Ltd jointly undertaking construction, supervision, financing, regulatory compliance and marketing for the 33 Holland Link project.
On 11 January 2010, the issued share capital of Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd was increased to S$1 million through cash injections from Ms Annie Chen Liping and Mr Wayne Yang.
On 18 February 2010, Mr Ow Chun Ming was recorded in the Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority records as a director of Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd.
On 24 February 2010, 500,000 shares, representing 50% of the issued share capital of Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd, were transferred to Mr Ow Chun Ming.
After the 24 February 2010 share transfer, the shareholders of Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd were Ms Annie Chen Liping with 40%, Mr Ow Chun Ming with 50%, and Mr Wayne Yang with 10%.

In January 2010 email correspondence, Ms Annie Chen Liping expressed concern to Mr Ow Chun Ming that Fong Yun Thai Association might not proceed with Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd for the 33 Holland Link project, and he responded that they were close to an agreement and that he was seeking a bankable joint venture agreement.

On 27 March 2010, Mr Leow Soon Guan emailed Ms Annie Chen Liping attaching a draft termination agreement intended to be executed by Tianjin Heping Construction Group Co Ltd and Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd regarding the 33 Holland Link project.
On 7 September 2010, Ms Annie Chen Liping emailed Mr Ow Chun Ming describing her earlier decision to work with him on the 33 Holland Link project and expressing distress about subsequent events.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, before any dispute arose, Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd had a concrete and ongoing business opportunity to act as developer for the 33 Holland Link project and how the involvement of its director-shareholder, Mr Ow Chun Ming, affected that opportunity.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors of a company are generally act in the interests of the company, avoid situations where their personal interests conflict with the company’s interests, and not appropriate for themselves business opportunities that the company is actively pursuing.
Party_Details.Application: Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd, controlled in decision-making by Ms Annie Chen Liping, moved from event organisation into property development and became involved in the 33 Holland Link project through the Cooperation Agreement between Tianjin Heping Construction Group Co Ltd and Fong Yun Thai Association and the subsequent draft Joint Venture Agreement naming Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd as developer. Excavation works were commenced by China Heping Construction (Far East) Pte Ltd, and further joint venture drafts were exchanged. In late 2009, Ms Chen engaged with Mr Ow Chun Ming, an experienced developer, who proposed by email on 4 December 2009 to become a 50% shareholder in Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd and to have his group jointly undertake the project with Tianjin Heping Construction Group Co Ltd. Following this, the company’s capital was increased to S$1 million, and Mr Ow became both a director and 50% shareholder in February 2010. During this period, Ms Chen relied on Mr Ow’s industry experience and contacts in relation to the project, while Fong Yun Thai Association’s internal committees and lawyers continued to work on documentation and project arrangements with the plaintiff and related entities.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analysed whether the 33 Holland Link project was a maturing corporate opportunity of the plaintiff, whether the plaintiff was actively pursuing it, and whether Mr Ow’s resignation and subsequent tender for the project constituted a breach of fiduciary duties. It considered Canadian Aero Service Ltd v O’Malley and related authorities on corporate opportunities, emphasising that a director may be accountable even after resignation if he resigns to appropriate a maturing opportunity the company is pursuing. The judge rejected the defendants’ argument that Mr Ow’s fiduciary duties were narrowly confined to a future joint venture and found that he owed the usual fiduciary duties of a director. The court found that the project was a concretised opportunity, not merely speculative, and that the plaintiff had invested resources and was still pursuing it through negotiations and draft agreements. The judge held that the likelihood of the plaintiff ultimately securing the project was irrelevant to liability, following Industrial Development Consultants Ltd v Cooley and Regal (Hastings) Ltd v Gulliver. The court concluded that Mr Ow’s resignation was prompted by his wish to acquire the project for himself, noting his rapid engagement with Fong Yun Thai Association, his participation in the URA meeting as the proposed new developer, and his tender for the project while still a director. The judge did not accept that Ms Chen had consented to or ratified any breach, and further held that any ratification would have required shareholder approval, including consideration of the position of minority shareholder Mr Wayne Yang. On the inducement of breach of contract claim, the court found that there was no binding contract between the plaintiff and Fong Yun Thai Association based on the Cooperation Agreement and the unexecuted Joint Venture Agreement, and that the first defendant did not procure a breach. The alleged profit-sharing agreement of 25 July 2010 was rejected on the evidence. For knowing receipt, the court considered whether a diverted corporate opportunity could constitute trust property and examined authorities such as CMS Dolphin Ltd v Simonet, Swiss Butchery Pte Ltd v Huber Ernst, and Ultraframe (UK) Ltd v Fielding, before determining the second defendant’s accessory liability and appropriate remedies.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Ow Chun Ming

Facts.Text: Mr Ow Chun Ming, also known as Mr Victor Ow, is a real estate developer with approximately 30 years of experience in the industry and is the Chairman and CEO of the Clydesbuilt Group of companies.
Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd was incorporated on 17 May 2010 as a company within the Clydesbuilt Group for the purpose of developing the 33 Holland Link project.
Around 2006, Ms Annie Chen Liping met Mr Ow Chun Ming through a mutual friend when she expressed interest in being a subcontractor for one of his construction projects.
Around 2007, Mr Ow Chun Ming sought Ms Annie Chen Liping’s assistance for a contact in Tianjin, China, in relation to difficulties in one of his projects there.

In September 2009, while driving home past the 33 Holland Link site where excavation works were underway, Mr Ow Chun Ming saw Ms Annie Chen Liping supervising the works and stopped to speak with her about the project.
After the initial encounter at the 33 Holland Link site in or around September 2009, Ms Annie Chen Liping and Mr Ow Chun Ming met at his home to discuss the project in more detail.
During discussions at his home in late 2009, Ms Annie Chen Liping showed Mr Ow Chun Ming the English-language Joint Venture Agreement relating to the 33 Holland Link project.

On 4 December 2009, Mr Ow Chun Ming emailed Ms Annie Chen Liping proposing that he personally would hold 50% of the shares in Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd, with Clydesbuilt Group and Tianjin Heping Construction Group Co Ltd jointly undertaking development, construction, supervision, financing, regulatory compliance, marketing and sales for the 33 Holland Link project.

In December 2009, following agreement in principle with Ms Annie Chen Liping, Mr Ow Chun Ming signed a consent to act as director of Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd and a share transfer form as transferee of half of the company’s issued share capital.
On 18 February 2010, records at the Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority reflected Mr Ow Chun Ming as a director of Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd.
On 24 February 2010, 500,000 shares in Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd, representing 50% of its issued share capital, were transferred to Mr Ow Chun Ming.
On 1 January 2010, Mr Ow Chun Ming left Singapore for a five-week holiday in Paris while negotiations between Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd and Fong Yun Thai Association continued.

In January and February 2010, email exchanges between Ms Annie Chen Liping and Mr Ow Chun Ming discussed Fong Yun Thai Association’s changing attitude to the 33 Holland Link project and the need to secure a bankable joint venture agreement and project financing.

On 24 February 2010, Ms Annie Chen Liping introduced Mr Ow Chun Ming at a lunch at Raffles Town Club to representatives of Fong Yun Thai Association, including Mr Leow Soon Guan, as a developer and potential partner for the 33 Holland Link project.
After the 24 February 2010 lunch, Mr Ow Chun Ming showed Fong Yun Thai Association representatives the completed residential development known as Lornie 18 at 18 Lornie Road.
Approximately two days after the 24 February 2010 lunch, Fong Yun Thai Association representatives, including Mr Leow Soon Guan, visited Mr Ow Chun Ming at his bungalow near the 33 Holland Link site.

On 2 March 2010, Mr Ow Chun Ming attended a meeting at the Urban Redevelopment Authority with Mr Leow Soon Guan and architect Mr Ho Seow Hui to seek a six-month extension of the Provisional Permission for the 33 Holland Link project.

At the 2 March 2010 Urban Redevelopment Authority meeting, Mr Leow Soon Guan presented Mr Ow Chun Ming and architect Mr Ho Seow Hui as the proposed new development team for the 33 Holland Link project.
Following the 2 March 2010 meeting, the Urban Redevelopment Authority granted a further six-month extension of the Provisional Permission for the 33 Holland Link project.

Between 18 March 2010 and 24 August 2010, on instructions from Mr Ow Chun Ming, his lawyer Ms Maria Anne Ng conducted multiple Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority searches on the directorship records of Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd.

In early April 2010, Fong Yun Thai Association representative Mr Leow Soon Guan obtained an Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority search showing that Mr Ow Chun Ming was a director and shareholder of Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd and subsequently asked him to explain this involvement.
On or around 7 April 2010, Mr Ow Chun Ming received an official invitation from Fong Yun Thai Association to tender for the role of developer for the 33 Holland Link project.
On 19 April 2010, Mr Ow Chun Ming submitted a tender proposal to Fong Yun Thai Association to develop the 33 Holland Link project.
On 4 May 2010, the board of Fong Yun Thai Association accepted the proposal submitted by Mr Ow Chun Ming for the 33 Holland Link project.

On 17 May 2010, Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd was incorporated as a special-purpose company to carry out the 33 Holland Link project, with Mr Ow Chun Ming and his brother appointed as initial directors.

On 20 October 2010, four additional directors were appointed to the board of Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd, including three representatives from Fong Yun Thai Association and a sister of Mr Ow Chun Ming.

On 20 October 2010, Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd entered into a sale and purchase agreement with the trustees of Fong Yun Thai Association to acquire the 33 Holland Link land for S$70 million, with payment to be effected by set-off against Fong Yun Thai Association’s share of project development costs.

On 7 October 2010, a joint venture agreement was entered into among Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd, Clydesbuilt Investment Pte Ltd and Fong Yun Thai Association, under which Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd had paid-up capital of S$1 million from Clydesbuilt Investment Pte Ltd and Fong Yun Thai Association representatives were issued 1,000,000 preference shares at S$0.01 each.

Under the 7 October 2010 joint venture arrangement, Fong Yun Thai Association was entitled to 25 of the 82 residential units to be built at 33 Holland Link, and Clydesbuilt Investment Pte Ltd was entitled to 57 units.
The 33 Holland Link residential project undertaken by Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd was completed sometime in 2014.

On 26 February 2018, Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd and Fong Yun Thai Association entered into a sale and purchase agreement for the transfer of 21 residential units at 33 Holland Link to Fong Yun Thai Association.
On 31 July 2018, 21 residential units at 33 Holland Link were transferred from Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd to Fong Yun Thai Association, in addition to four units that had been sold earlier.

After the 31 July 2018 transfer of units, the 1,000,000 preference shares in Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd held by Fong Yun Thai Association were cancelled and its representatives resigned as directors, leaving Clydesbuilt Investment Pte Ltd as the sole shareholder.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, before any dispute arose, Mr Ow Chun Ming’s role as an experienced developer, director and 50% shareholder of Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd, and his subsequent establishment and use of Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd for the 33 Holland Link project, created a conflict between his personal project participation and the company’s existing project opportunity.
Party_Details.Rule: Individuals who become directors and significant shareholders of a company are generally use project information and counterpart relationships obtained in that capacity for the company’s benefit, and to avoid situations where they personally pursue the same defined project opportunity through separate vehicles while still holding office in the company.
Party_Details.Application: By late 2009, Mr Ow Chun Ming had detailed knowledge of the 33 Holland Link project through discussions with Ms Annie Chen Liping and review of the Joint Venture Agreement. He proposed to become a 50% shareholder in Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd and to have his group jointly undertake the project with Tianjin Heping Construction Group Co Ltd. In December 2009 he signed documents to become a director and transferee of half the company’s shares, and by February 2010 he was recorded as a director and 50% shareholder. Ms Chen introduced him to Fong Yun Thai Association’s leadership on 24 February 2010 in the context of the plaintiff’s existing project arrangements, and he then accompanied them to view his completed development and hosted them at his nearby home. On 2 March 2010 he attended the Urban Redevelopment Authority meeting with Fong Yun Thai Association’s new project chairman and his own architect, where they presented a new development team and obtained an extension of Provisional Permission. Within weeks, he received a formal invitation to tender, submitted his own bid on 19 April 2010, and had his proposal accepted on 4 May 2010. He then incorporated Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd on 17 May 2010 as the project vehicle, entered into a joint venture and land acquisition structure with Fong Yun Thai Association in October 2010, and oversaw completion and unit allocation under that structure.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court examined whether Mr Ow’s conduct amounted to a diversion of a maturing corporate opportunity belonging to Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd. It held that he owed full fiduciary duties as a director, rejecting the argument that his duties were limited to a future joint venture. Applying Canadian Aero Service Ltd v O’Malley, the judge found that the 33 Holland Link project was a maturing business opportunity that the plaintiff was actively pursuing through the Cooperation Agreement, excavation works, and ongoing joint venture negotiations. The court concluded that Mr Ow’s resignation was prompted by his wish to acquire the project personally, noting his rapid engagement with Fong Yun Thai Association, his role at the URA meeting as the proposed new developer, and his tender submission while still a director. The judge rejected his account of an early undated resignation and found that his formal resignation occurred only in August 2010, after his tender had been accepted. The court did not accept that Ms Chen had consented to his independent pursuit of the project or that any shareholder ratification had occurred, particularly in light of minority shareholder Mr Wayne Yang’s position. On the inducement of breach of contract claim, the court held that there was no binding contract between the plaintiff and Fong Yun Thai Association based on the Cooperation Agreement and unexecuted Joint Venture Agreement, and that Mr Ow did not procure a breach. The alleged 25 July 2010 profit-sharing agreement was rejected due to evidential weaknesses. In assessing accessory liability of Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd, the court considered whether the diverted corporate opportunity and resulting contractual rights constituted trust property and applied authorities such as CMS Dolphin Ltd v Simonet, Swiss Butchery Pte Ltd v Huber Ernst, and Ultraframe (UK) Ltd v Fielding to determine whether knowing receipt or dishonest assistance was established.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte. Ltd.

Facts.Text: Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd was incorporated on 17 May 2010 within the Clydesbuilt Group for the purpose of developing the 33 Holland Link residential project.

Under a joint venture agreement dated 7 October 2010, Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd had paid-up share capital of S$1 million provided by its sole shareholder Clydesbuilt Investment Pte Ltd, and representatives of Fong Yun Thai Association were issued 1,000,000 preference shares at S$0.01 each.
At incorporation on 17 May 2010, Mr Ow Chun Ming and his brother were appointed as directors of Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd.

On 20 October 2010, four additional directors were appointed to the board of Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd, including three representatives from Fong Yun Thai Association and a sister of Mr Ow Chun Ming.

On 7 October 2010, Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd, Clydesbuilt Investment Pte Ltd and Fong Yun Thai Association entered into a joint venture agreement for the development of 82 residential units and a Hakka Memorial Museum and Cultural Centre at 33 Holland Link.

Under the 7 October 2010 joint venture arrangement, Fong Yun Thai Association was entitled to 25 of the 82 residential units at 33 Holland Link and Clydesbuilt Investment Pte Ltd was entitled to 57 units.

On 20 October 2010, Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd entered into a sale and purchase agreement with the trustees of Fong Yun Thai Association to acquire the 33 Holland Link land for S$70 million, with the price to be set off against Fong Yun Thai Association’s share of development costs.
The 33 Holland Link residential project undertaken by Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd was completed sometime in 2014.

On 26 February 2018, Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd and Fong Yun Thai Association entered into a sale and purchase agreement for the transfer of 21 residential units at 33 Holland Link to Fong Yun Thai Association.
On 31 July 2018, 21 residential units at 33 Holland Link were transferred from Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd to Fong Yun Thai Association, in addition to four units that had been sold earlier.

After the 31 July 2018 transfer of units, the 1,000,000 preference shares in Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd held by Fong Yun Thai Association were cancelled and its representatives resigned as directors, leaving Clydesbuilt Investment Pte Ltd as the sole shareholder.

As at mid-2018, the board of Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd comprised Mr Ow Chun Ming, his daughter and his brother, and the company held 48 of its 57 earmarked residential units at 33 Holland Link, with nine units sold.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, before any dispute arose, Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd’s role as the special-purpose vehicle for the 33 Holland Link project, funded and controlled through the Clydesbuilt Group and joint venture with Fong Yun Thai Association, intersected with a pre-existing project opportunity associated with Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: A company that is established as a project vehicle and receives project rights and assets through joint venture and land acquisition arrangements may be examined for whether those rights and assets are connected to opportunities or information previously associated with another company through that company’s directors.
Party_Details.Application: Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd was incorporated on 17 May 2010 shortly after Fong Yun Thai Association’s board accepted a tender proposal submitted by Mr Ow Chun Ming for the 33 Holland Link project. The company was capitalised with S$1 million from Clydesbuilt Investment Pte Ltd and structured under an October 2010 joint venture in which Fong Yun Thai Association received preference shares and an entitlement to 25 residential units, while Clydesbuilt Investment Pte Ltd was allocated 57 units. On 20 October 2010, Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd acquired the project land for S$70 million via a sale and purchase agreement with Fong Yun Thai Association’s trustees, with payment to be set off against development cost allocations. The board composition included Mr Ow and his relatives alongside Fong Yun Thai Association representatives until 2018, when the association’s preference shares were cancelled and its directors resigned following transfer of 21 units. These arrangements positioned Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd as the holder of the project land and the majority of units for the 33 Holland Link development that had earlier been the subject of negotiations involving Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd and related entities.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court considered whether Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd was liable on the bases of knowing receipt and dishonest assistance in relation to a diverted corporate opportunity. It analysed whether the 33 Holland Link project opportunity and resulting contractual rights and land interests could be treated as trust property of Innovative Corporation Pte Ltd, referring to CMS Dolphin Ltd v Simonet, Swiss Butchery Pte Ltd v Huber Ernst, and Ultraframe (UK) Ltd v Fielding. The judge applied the three-part test for knowing receipt from George Raymond Zage III v Ho Chi Kwong, examining whether there had been a disposal of the plaintiff’s assets in breach of fiduciary duty, whether Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd had beneficially received assets traceable to that breach, and whether it had the requisite knowledge making retention unconscionable. The court also addressed the dishonest assistance claim, focusing on whether Clydesbuilt (Holland Link) Pte Ltd, through the knowledge and actions of its controlling individuals, participated in Mr Ow’s breach of fiduciary duties. It evaluated the timing of the company’s incorporation, the joint venture and land acquisition structure, and the involvement of Fong Yun Thai Association’s representatives on the board in determining the extent of any accessory liability and the appropriate remedial response.
